timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — Jehoiakim Burns Scroll

sections.header: Verses 1-5 -- "Do You Seek Great Things for Yourself?"

heb.paragraph: "Do you seek great things (gedolot) for yourself? Do not seek them" (v.5). The prohibition is not against ambition in general but against personal advancement when God is bringing "disaster on all people." In a world under judgment, the appropriate ambition is survival, not greatness. Baruch's lament -- "Woe to me! The LORD has added sorrow to my pain" (v.3) -- echoes Jeremiah's confessions: the scribe shares the prophet's suffering.

refs.note: Ebed-Melech receives the same promise: "your life as plunder." Both the faithful servant and the faithful scribe are promised survival, not prosperity.
"Whoever wants to save their life will lose it" -- the NT parallel of the relinquishment ethic.
Jeremiah's own confession: Baruch's lament mirrors the prophet's. Master and scribe share the same suffering.

notes.note: The date -- fourth year of Jehoiakim -- is the year of the scroll dictation (ch. 36). Baruch's grief may stem from the emotional toll of writing down forty years of judgment oracles.
"I am worn out with groaning and find no rest." Baruch's confession echoes Jeremiah's confessions: the cost of serving the prophetic word falls on the scribe as well as the prophet.

God acknowledges his own grief: "I will overthrow what I have built and uproot what I have planted." Even God laments the destruction. In this context, personal ambition is inappropriate -- but life is guaranteed.

Calvin reads this as universal pastoral counsel: "In times of divine judgment, seek survival, not status. The wise person adjusts ambitions to the season.". Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: "Your life as plunder" (nephesh leshallal) -- the same idiom used in 21:9 and 39:18. In military context, "plunder" is what a soldier carries away from a battle. Baruch's only spoil will be his own survival. The NET Bible's textual notes draw attention to variant readings and translation challenges in this passage. The translators weigh manuscript evidence alongside syntactic analysis to arrive at renderings that balance accuracy with readability, providing supplementary notes where the original resists simple translation.

lundbom.source: Jack R. Lundbom, Jeremiah, Anchor Bible (3 vols., 1999–2004) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Lundbom identifies chapter 45 as "the colophon of the Baruch narrative." It serves the same function as a scribal postscript -- authenticating the scribe's role and acknowledging his suffering. This observation invites further reflection on the passage's contribution to the book's theological argument. The text operates on multiple levels simultaneously — historical, literary, and theological — and each level enriches the others when the passage is read with attention to its canonical context.

brueggemann.source: Walter Brueggemann, Jeremiah, ITC (1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Brueggemann reads God's self-disclosure as "the most intimate divine speech in the book." God reveals his own grief at destroying what he built. The creator mourns his own creation. In this context, Baruch's personal ambition is gently but firmly relativized.

hist.context: This brief chapter, dated to the fourth year of Jehoiakim (605 BC), is a personal oracle to Baruch. It is placed here as a coda to the narrative section (chs. 26-44) and a bridge to the oracles against the nations (chs. 46-51). Baruch's complaint and God's response encapsulate the entire book in miniature: lament meets divine purpose, and the promise is not comfort but life itself -- "your life as plunder" (v.5), the same phrase used for Ebed-Melech (39:18).

themes.note: Sovereignty at 8 -- God destroys what he built, and the appropriate human response is surrender of ambition. Faith at 6: Baruch trusts despite sorrow.

rows.label: vv.1
rows.text: Date and Setting: The Fourth Year

lit.note: The shortest chapter in the section serves as a personal coda and theological summary: in a world under judgment, the promise is life, not greatness.

The LORD has added sorrow to my pain" (v.3) -- echoes Jeremiah's confessions: the scribe shares the prophet's suffering.

trans.title: Comparing translations in Oracle to Baruch

translations.text: When Baruch son of Neriah wrote on a scroll the words Jeremiah the prophet dictated in the fourth year of Jehoiakim son of Josiah king of Judah, Jeremiah said this to Baruch:

The word that Jeremiah the prophet spoke to Baruch the son of Neriah, when he wrote these words in a book at the dictation of Jeremiah, in the fourth year of Jehoiakim the son of Josiah, king of Judah:
Should you then seek great things for yourself? Do not seek them. For I will bring disaster on all people, declares the Lord, but wherever you go I will let you escape with your life.’ ”

And do you seek great things for yourself? Seek them not, for behold, I am bringing disaster upon all flesh, declares the LORD. But I will give you your life as a prize of war in all places to which you may go.”

"Whoever wants to save their life will lose it" -- the NT parallel of the relinquishment ethic.
Jeremiah's own confession: Baruch's lament mirrors the prophet's. Master and scribe share the same suffering.

coaching.tip: 'Do you seek great things for yourself? Do not seek them' (v. 5). This tiny chapter — five verses — is addressed to Baruch, Jeremiah's scribe. In a collapsing world, personal ambition is beside the point. But God adds a promise: 'I will let you escape with your life.' In a book where cities burn and kings are blinded, survival is the great thing. Baruch's chapter recalibrates the meaning of success.
